ME.intro: Montenegro is one of the most keenly priced coastal markets on the Adriatic: it uses the euro, and foreign buyers may purchase an apartment or a house on effectively the same terms as locals. Property transfer tax has been banded since 2024: 3% up to €150,000, 5% above that, and 6% above €500,000. Demand is concentrated in Budva, Kotor, Tivat (Porto Montenegro) and Herceg Novi; sea-view apartments in the Bay of Kotor are the most sought-after investment product, while Bar and Ulcinj still offer a materially cheaper entry point. The country is an EU candidate, which is a long-term upward driver of prices.
highlights: The euro is the official currency — no exchange-rate risk on the purchase price
Banded transfer tax since 2024: 3% / 5% / 6% (thresholds at €150,000 and €500,000)
No transfer tax on new-build — 21% VAT is included in the price instead
Foreign individuals may freely buy apartments, houses and building plots
There is NO value threshold for EU citizens seeking residence (€150,000 for third-country nationals since 2026)
EU candidate country — long-term appreciation potential

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy real estate in Montenegro?
faq.a: Yes. A foreign private individual may freely buy a flat, house and building plot in Montenegro, in their own name, without a special permit. Restrictions apply primarily to agricultural and forest land — these are usually bought through a local company.

faq.q: What are the ancillary costs of buying real estate in Montenegro?
faq.a: The transfer tax has been banded since 2024: up to 150 000 € it is 3%; between 150 000 and 500 000 € it is 4 500 € plus 5% of the part above 150 000 €; above 500 000 € it is 22 000 € plus 6% of the part above 500 000 €. Its base is the market value determined by the tax authority, not necessarily the contract price. Added to this are the notary fee, roughly 1% lawyer's fee, and the agent's commission (typically 3%, customarily paid by the seller). On the first sale of a newly built property constructed after 2003 there is no transfer tax — there is 21% VAT in the price instead.

faq.q: Does Montenegro grant a residence permit for buying real estate?
faq.a: Yes, on the basis of property ownership a one-year residence permit, renewable annually, can be applied for. From 17 January 2026 a third-country citizen needs property with a tax-authority value of at least 150 000 € for this — but EU, EEA and Swiss citizens ARE NOT SUBJECT TO THIS VALUE LIMIT, so a Hungarian buyer has no minimum. The former citizenship-by-investment programme closed on 31 December 2022, so today you cannot obtain citizenship through real estate in Montenegro — through real estate a route is offered primarily by Turkey.

HR.intro: Croatia is the mature, EU-member market of the Adriatic: since 2023 it has been part of both the eurozone and the Schengen area, which makes it the lowest-friction coastal purchase available to Hungarian and other EU buyers. As an individual, an EU citizen buys on the same terms as locals — the ministerial consent formerly required of foreigners is no longer needed. Istria (Rovinj, Poreč) drives the near-town segment, Dalmatia (Split, Trogir, Šibenik) the holiday-home segment, and Dubrovnik the premium end. Yields from tourist letting are high because of seasonality, but heavily concentrated in the summer.
highlights: EU and eurozone member state — no consent requirement for EU citizens
Property transfer tax is a flat 3%; on new-build, 25% VAT is included in the price instead
Low notary costs: the notary only certifies signatures (a few euros) and does not draft the contract
Since 2025 there is also an ANNUAL property tax: €0.60–8.00 per m² per year, set by each municipality
The moratorium on agricultural land expired on 30 June 2023 — EU buyers may now purchase freely
A strong but highly seasonal short-term letting market

faq.q: Can a Hungarian citizen buy property in Croatia?
faq.a: Yes, without restriction. As both Hungary and Croatia are EU member states, a Hungarian individual may buy an apartment or house in their own name on the same terms as Croatian citizens — no consent from the Ministry of Justice is required. The transitional moratorium on agricultural land expired on 30 June 2023 and was not extended, so as an EU citizen you may now also buy farmland on the same terms as Croatians. Third-country buyers still require reciprocity and ministerial consent.

faq.q: How much tax is payable on a Croatian property purchase?
faq.a: On a second-hand property the buyer pays 3% property transfer tax (porez na promet nekretnina) — a flat rate, not banded. On a new-build bought from a VAT-registered developer there is no transfer tax, because the price includes 25% VAT; the two are mutually exclusive. Importantly, the Croatian notary does NOT draft the sale contract, but merely certifies the seller's signature, so notary costs amount to a few euros plus the land registry fee — the '1% notary fee' customary elsewhere is simply wrong here. Legal fees are typically 1–1.5% plus VAT, and agency commission 2–4%. Since 2025 ownership also attracts an annual property tax (€0.60–8.00 per m² per year), from which registered permanent residence and long-term letting of at least 10 months are exempt.

faq.q: Is it worth buying property in Croatia for short-term letting?
faq.a: On the Dalmatian and Istrian coast the summer yield is outstanding, but the season is typically limited to 3–4 months, and tourist letting requires a categorisation decision (rješenje o kategorizaciji) and flat-rate taxation. On an annual basis, net yields typically fall between 3% and 5%.

AL.intro: Albania has the lowest entry threshold of any Mediterranean coastal market: on the Albanian Riviera (Sarandë, Ksamil, Himarë) the per-square-metre price of new-build sea-view apartments is still a fraction of Croatian or Greek levels, while Corfu and the Greek border are a stone's throw away. There is effectively no property transfer tax, and foreign buyers may purchase apartments in their own name. Tourism has grown at record rates in recent years, lifting short-term letting yields into the top tier of the region — in exchange, the market is younger, and legal due diligence (title, building permit) matters here more than anywhere.
highlights: The lowest coastal per-square-metre prices in the region
The BUYER pays no ad valorem transfer tax — only a fixed notary charge and registration fee
No VAT on the buyer's side for new-build either: property sales are VAT-exempt
Foreign individuals may buy apartments and houses in their own name, without consent
To buy a building PLOT, the value of the development must exceed three times the value of the land
Foreigners may not buy agricultural land, only lease it

faq.q: Is it safe to buy property in Albania as a foreigner?
faq.a: Legally, yes — a foreign individual may buy an apartment in their own name, and ownership can be registered with the cadastre (ASHK). The risk lies not in acquiring title but in the history behind it: the Albanian market does contain properties with unresolved title or built without permits, so never pay a deposit here without due diligence carried out by an independent lawyer.

faq.q: How much does a coastal apartment in Albania cost?
faq.a: In Ksamil and Sarandë, new-build sea-view apartments typically range from €1,200 to €2,200 per m², and entry prices in Vlorë and Durrës are lower still. That is roughly a half to a third of comparable Croatian or Greek stock. Buyer's transaction costs are also the lowest in the region: with no ad valorem transfer tax and no VAT, they typically come to 1–4% of the purchase price.

faq.q: Does Albania grant residence in exchange for buying property?
faq.a: Albania has neither a Golden Visa programme nor a citizenship-by-investment programme: the option set out in the 2020 legislation was never launched, has been under a moratorium since March 2023, and following the Court of Justice of the EU's April 2025 ruling on Malta, Albania — a country preparing for EU accession — is unlikely to open it. Property ownership does, however, support an application for a renewable one-year residence permit, leading to permanent residence after five years. The '€100,000 threshold' cited by agents cannot be traced to any legislation — do not plan around it.

RS.intro: Serbia is primarily an urban rental market rather than a coastal holiday market. The residential markets of Belgrade (particularly Belgrade Waterfront, Vračar and Novi Beograd) and Novi Sad are kept tight by strong internal migration, a growing IT sector and large numbers of arriving foreign workers — which means stable, year-round long-term rental demand, in contrast to the seasonality of Adriatic markets. The mountain resorts (Zlatibor, Kopaonik) are the terrain for two-season apartment investment. Transfer tax is 2.5%, replaced by VAT on new-build.
highlights: Year-round, non-seasonal rental demand in Belgrade and Novi Sad
Property transfer tax of 2.5% on second-hand property (by law payable by the SELLER, though contracts typically pass it to the buyer)
On new-build, 10% VAT (residential property) is included in the price instead
Foreign buyers may purchase on the basis of RECIPROCITY — this is satisfied for Hungarian citizens, but must be checked country by country
First-home reliefs are available only to Serbian citizens — foreign buyers cannot claim them
A two-season mountain apartment market (Zlatibor, Kopaonik)

faq.q: Can a Hungarian citizen buy property in Serbia?
faq.a: Yes. Serbia permits property acquisition by foreign individuals on the basis of reciprocity, and reciprocity with Hungary is in place (Hungary appears on the ministry's list), so a Hungarian citizen may buy an apartment or house in their own name. Importantly, reciprocity is assessed COUNTRY BY COUNTRY, not at EU level — so it is not true that 'any EU citizen may buy freely'. EU individuals are in principle entitled to buy agricultural land, but subject to cumulative conditions (10 years of local residence, 3 years of cultivation, a 2-hectare cap) that are all but impossible to meet in practice.

faq.q: What rental yield can be achieved in Belgrade?
faq.a: Gross yields on long-term residential letting in Belgrade typically run between 4% and 6%, higher than in most EU capitals, and demand is year-round — it does not depend on the tourist season.

faq.q: What does buying property in Serbia cost?
faq.a: On second-hand property, transfer tax is 2.5% (porez na prenos apsolutnih prava). The law imposes it on the SELLER, but contracts almost always shift it to the buyer — the seller nonetheless remains liable, so it is worth verifying that the payment has been made. On the first sale of a new-build completed after 2005, VAT applies in place of transfer tax: 10% on residential property, 20% on garages and commercial premises. Notary fees follow a banded tariff (a few hundred euros on an average apartment — the '1–2% notary fee' figure is wrong), plus legal fees and 2–4% agency commission. Only Serbian citizens qualify for the first-home tax exemption and VAT refund.

TR.intro: Turkey operates the world's highest-volume property-based citizenship programme: by buying property worth USD 400,000 and holding it for three years, the investor and their family can obtain Turkish CITIZENSHIP — not merely a residence permit — typically within 3 to 6 months and with no residence requirement. The Turkish passport gives visa-free entry to roughly 118 countries and opens the route to the US E-2 investor visa. The market rests on two legs: Istanbul (urban letting and capital growth) and the Mediterranean coast (Antalya, Alanya, Bodrum, Fethiye) for holiday and rental use. Transfer tax is 4%, and fixing the purchase price in foreign currency is mandatory under the programme.
highlights: USD 400,000 property purchase → Turkish CITIZENSHIP within 3–6 months
No residence, language or settlement requirement
The whole family (spouse + children under 18) can be included
The property must be held for 3 years, after which it can be sold freely
Visa-free entry to roughly 118 countries; a route to the US E-2 investor visa

faq.q: How can Turkish citizenship be obtained by buying property?
faq.a: You must buy Turkish property worth at least USD 400,000 and undertake not to sell it for three years — this is recorded on the title deed (tapu) as a 'satılamaz' (not for sale) annotation. Importantly, what counts is not the contract price but the official valuation by an appraiser licensed by the SPK; the threshold must be met by the price actually paid (evidenced through a bank), by the valuation, and by the value stated on the tapu alike. The residence permit and citizenship applications can then be submitted; the full process typically takes 3–6 months and extends to the spouse and children under 18.

faq.q: Do you have to live in Turkey to obtain citizenship?
faq.a: No. The Turkish citizenship-by-investment programme has no residence, language or examination requirement — you do not need to move to the country, and most of the application can in fact be handled by an authorised lawyer.

faq.q: Can I sell the property after obtaining citizenship?
faq.a: Yes, but only once the mandatory three-year holding period has elapsed. Citizenship is retained thereafter — Turkish citizenship is not revoked because the property has been sold.

faq.q: What are the transaction costs of buying property in Turkey?
faq.a: 4% tapu (transfer) duty, roughly 0.5% in notary and translation fees, legal fees of around 1.2%, and typically 2% agency commission. Under the citizenship programme you must add the mandatory valuation and the official processing fees.

ID.intro: Bali is a strong short-term letting market, but the real numbers are far more modest than advertised yields suggest: independent booking data put the average daily rate in Canggu at around USD 216, while occupancy is only about 38% and supply is expanding by 40% a year. Realistically, you should budget for 6–9% gross and 3–5% net, not the 10–15% quoted in brochures. The other key question is the form of ownership: a foreign individual CANNOT acquire freehold (Hak Milik) title in Indonesia. There are three legal routes: a contractual leasehold right (Hak Sewa, typically 25–30 years), a Hak Pakai right of use tied to a residence permit (30 + 20 + 30 = up to 80 years), or a Hak Guna Bangunan right to build held through an Indonesian PT PMA company (likewise 80 years). In Bali, choosing the legal structure matters more than choosing the property.
highlights: Realistic yields based on independent data: 6–9% gross, 3–5% net (10–15% is a marketing figure)
Foreigners CANNOT acquire freehold (Hak Milik) title — this is a constitutional restriction
Hak Sewa leasehold right: available without a residence permit, typically 25–30 years
Hak Pakai and PT PMA-held Hak Guna Bangunan: 30 + 20 + 30, up to 80 years in total
DANGER: the 'nominee' structure (freehold registered in an Indonesian proxy's name) is NULL AND VOID UNDER THE LAW
If a Hak Pakai owner's residence permit expires, they must sell the property within one year
Buyer's transaction costs: 2–4% on a leasehold right, 7–9% on Hak Pakai (because of 5% BPHTB)

faq.q: Does Indonesia grant residence in exchange for buying property?
faq.a: Only in a limited way. The property-based branch of the Indonesian Golden Visa requires an investment of USD 1,000,000 and applies EXCLUSIVELY to strata-title apartments with appropriate title — villas on individual plots do not qualify. There is also a 'second home' visa, but the official site publishes no specific value threshold, so the figures quoted by agents should be treated with caution. Indonesia has no citizenship-by-investment programme.

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy property in Bali?
faq.a: A foreign individual cannot acquire full freehold (Hak Milik) title in Indonesia — the 1960 basic law reserves it for Indonesian citizens. There are three legal solutions: a contractual leasehold right (Hak Sewa, typically 25–30 years, with no residence permit required), a Hak Pakai right of use (tied to a valid KITAS/KITAP residence permit, 30 + 20 + 30 = up to 80 years), and a Hak Guna Bangunan right to build held by a foreign-owned Indonesian company (PT PMA, likewise 80 years). VERY IMPORTANT: the 'nominee' structure frequently sold in Bali, under which an Indonesian citizen holds the freehold 'for your benefit', is NULL AND VOID under the law — it is unenforceable in court, and the property remains with the proxy or with the state.

faq.q: What yield does a Bali villa produce?
faq.a: Less than advertised. According to independent booking data, in Canggu — where close to 4,000 active listings compete — the average daily rate is around USD 216 and annual occupancy is about 38%, which works out at average revenue of roughly USD 23,700 a year. From that you must deduct villa management (15–20%), maintenance and tax. Realistically, plan for 6–9% gross and 3–5% net. Promises of 10–15% net come from sales materials and are not borne out by market data — supply is growing at over 40% a year while demand is flat, so owners are conceding 10–30% off asking prices.

faq.q: What happens when the leasehold expires?
faq.a: A well-drafted lease contains an extension option at a pre-agreed or index-linked price. That is precisely the most important check before buying: how many years remain, what the extension right is worth, and how the landowner's consent is secured. Without this, the value of the investment declines year after year. Hak Pakai carries a further trap: if the owner's residence permit expires and is not renewed, they are obliged to sell the property within one year, failing which it may be put up for state auction.

HU.intro: Hungary relaunched its investor residence programme in 2024, but one important misconception needs clearing up: under the Guest Investor Programme, BUYING A HOME DIRECTLY DOES NOT QUALIFY for a residence permit. The planned €500,000 direct property option was deleted in January 2025 before it ever took effect. Two routes remain: €250,000 of units in a real estate fund registered with the Hungarian National Bank (MNB) whose net asset value is at least 40% invested in Hungarian residential property, with a five-year holding requirement; or a €1,000,000 non-refundable donation to a Hungarian higher education institution. The Hungarian property market has two engines, Budapest and Lake Balaton; the transfer duty on property acquired for consideration is 4% up to one billion forints.
highlights: ATTENTION: buying a home directly DOES NOT QUALIFY for a guest investor residence permit
Only real estate fund units (€250,000, 5-year holding) or a higher education donation (€1,000,000) count under the programme
The permit runs for 10 years and can be extended once by a further 10 years
Transfer duty on property acquired for consideration is 4% up to one billion forints and 2% above that (capped at HUF 200 million per property)
5% VAT on new homes (apartments up to 150 m², houses up to 300 m²) until the end of 2026
Budapest: year-round yields underpinned by student and tenant demand
Lake Balaton: a domestic holiday market in sustained appreciation

faq.q: Can I obtain Hungarian residence if I buy an apartment in Hungary?
faq.a: No. This is the most common misconception about the Hungarian programme. Under the Guest Investor Programme, buying an apartment or house directly does NOT count as an investment: the planned €500,000 direct property option was deleted in January 2025 before it took effect. There are two options: buying €250,000 of units in a real estate fund registered with the MNB that invests at least 40% in Hungarian residential property (with a 5-year holding requirement), or a €1,000,000 donation to a Hungarian higher education institution. The permit runs for 10 years and can be extended once by a further 10 years. Hungarian and EU citizens of course have no need for it — they enjoy freedom of movement.

faq.q: How much duty is payable on a Hungarian property purchase?
faq.a: The transfer duty on property acquired for consideration is 4% on the portion of market value up to one billion forints and 2% on the portion above, capped at HUF 200 million per property. On a new home bought from a developer, the duty is waived up to a value of HUF 15 million, with 4% payable on the excess above that. Reliefs or exemptions may be claimed by young first-time buyers, on purchases using CSOK, and where the sale and purchase take place within five years of each other — in which case duty is payable only on the price difference. VAT on new homes is 5% rather than 27%, for apartments up to 150 m² and houses up to 300 m², until the end of 2026.

faq.q: What rental yield is achievable in Budapest?
faq.a: Gross yields on long-term residential letting in Budapest are typically 4–6%, and can be higher on small flats in the centre near the universities. Short-term letting yields more, but is subject to rules that differ from district to district.

TH.intro: Of the South-East Asian markets, Thailand offers foreigners the most predictable ownership structure: a foreign individual may acquire a condominium unit IN THEIR OWN NAME, as freehold — provided that no more than 49% of the total area of the condominium in question is in foreign hands. Foreigners may not, however, own land or a house together with its land; there, a long-term lease or a corporate structure is customary. Bangkok is the urban letting market, Phuket and Koh Samui the tourist-yield markets, and Chiang Mai the market for long-staying digital nomads.
highlights: Condominium units can be acquired freehold, in your own name, as a foreigner
A 49% foreign quota per condominium, measured by FLOOR AREA — to be verified in writing before buying
The purchase price must be remitted from abroad in foreign currency (no transfer without an FET certificate)
Foreigners may not own land; the statutory maximum for a registrable lease is 30 years
The '30+30+30' extension is only a contractual promise — the courts have not enforced it against a new owner
Transfer fee of 2%, customarily split between the parties — the buyer's total cost is typically 1–2%
ATTENTION: the 0.01% reduced fee applies ONLY to Thai citizens, not to foreign buyers

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy property in Thailand?
faq.a: A condominium unit, yes — in your own name, as freehold — provided that no more than 49% of the building's registered saleable floor area is in foreign ownership. The purchase price must be remitted from abroad in foreign currency, and the Land Department will not register title without the FET certificate evidencing this. A foreign individual may not own land, or a house built on land; in such cases a lease registrable for a maximum of 30 years, a usufruct, or a Thai-majority company are the available options. Caution: a Thai-majority company is lawful only with genuine Thai owners — a nominee structure breaches the Foreign Business Act, and the authorities actively investigate it.

faq.q: Is it true that Thailand is raising the foreign quota to 75% and leases to 99 years?
faq.a: No, this is NOT the law at present. In 2024 the government approved only that the two proposals be examined; no legislation ever came of them. The 99-year lease plan was formally removed from the agenda in September 2025. As of mid-2026, the rule in force remains a 49% foreign quota and a 30-year maximum registrable lease. Anyone buying a property priced on a 99-year horizon is paying for a right that does not exist.

faq.q: What does the 49% foreign quota mean?
faq.a: In every Thai condominium, no more than 49% of the registered saleable floor area may be in foreign ownership — the limit is therefore proportional to floor area, not to the number of units. Before buying, you must obtain written confirmation from the condominium's management that there is still room within the quota; without it the unit cannot be transferred into a foreign buyer's name, and the deposit can easily become stuck.

faq.q: What are the transaction costs of buying in Thailand?
faq.a: A 2% transfer fee on the official assessed value, customarily split between the parties. The seller bears the specific business tax (3.3% where the property has been held for less than five years) or stamp duty instead (0.5% where held for at least five years), plus withholding tax. The parties' combined costs typically come to 6–8% of the purchase price, but under the customary split the FOREIGN BUYER's share is only about 1–2%, including legal and due diligence fees. Importantly, the much-cited 0.01% reduced transfer fee applies exclusively to Thai citizens — foreign buyers and Thai companies pay the full 2%.

IT.intro: Italy is the EU's deepest and most differentiated holiday and urban property market: from the premium segment of Lake Como and Tuscany, through Milan's rental market, to the '€1 house' schemes of the south, every price level is available. EU citizens may buy without restriction. Taxation is the key: transfer tax on a second-hand property bought from a private individual is 9% (for a second home), but can fall to 2% for a buyer who becomes resident, and those relocating from abroad can access the €200,000 annual flat tax on foreign income.
highlights: EU member state — no purchase restrictions for EU citizens
Transfer tax of 9% on a second home, 2% if it becomes your 'prima casa' (main home); minimum €1,000
When buying from a private individual, the tax base is the cadastral value (prezzo-valore), typically well below the purchase price
VAT on a new property bought from a developer: 4% main home, 10% second home, 22% luxury category
There is NO property-based Golden Visa — property is expressly excluded from the Italian investor visa
Flat tax on foreign income for new residents: €300,000 a year from 1 January 2026

faq.q: How much tax does a buyer pay on an Italian property purchase?
faq.a: On a second-hand property bought from a private individual, transfer tax (imposta di registro) is 9% where it is a second home, and only 2% where the buyer registers it as their main home within 18 months; the minimum in both cases is €1,000. In sales between private individuals, the tax base may on request be the cadastral value (prezzo-valore), which is typically well below the actual purchase price — a meaningful saving. To this are added two fixed charges of €50 each. On a new property bought from a developer within 5 years of completion, VAT applies to the purchase: 4% for a main home, 10% for a second home, and 22% in the A/1, A/8 and A/9 luxury cadastral categories, plus three fixed charges of €200 each.

faq.q: Can a Hungarian citizen buy property in Italy?
faq.a: Yes, without restriction — both countries are EU member states, so a Hungarian buyer purchases on the same terms as Italians. An Italian tax number (codice fiscale) and an Italian bank account are required for the purchase.

faq.q: What is the Italian flat tax for new residents?
faq.a: Anyone transferring their tax residence to Italy may elect a regime under which a fixed annual amount of tax is paid on their foreign-source income, for up to 15 years. The 2026 budget law raised the amount to €300,000 a year for those transferring their residence from 1 January 2026 (with a further €50,000 per family member). Those who entered earlier keep the old figure: €100,000 for entrants before 2024 and €200,000 for entrants in 2024–2025. Importantly, this is a tax construct, NOT a residence programme — buying Italian property does not in itself confer a right of residence.

GR.intro: Greece operates the EU's best-known Golden Visa programme: a property investment secures a five-year, renewable residence permit that covers the whole family and grants free movement within Schengen, WITH NO MINIMUM STAY REQUIREMENT — the programme's strongest argument. Since Law 5100/2024 the threshold has been banded by area: €800,000 across the whole Attica region, in Thessaloniki, on Mykonos and Santorini and on islands with populations above 3,100; €400,000 in the rest of the country; and €250,000 EXCLUSIVELY for converting a non-residential building into a dwelling or restoring a listed building. Two conditions are frequently left unmentioned: the property must be a single unit with at least 120 m² of main living area, and short-term (Airbnb-style) letting of a Golden Visa property is PROHIBITED — breaching the rule means revocation of the permit and a €50,000 fine.
highlights: Golden Visa bands: €800,000 (Attica, Thessaloniki, Mykonos, Santorini, larger islands) / €400,000 (elsewhere) / €250,000 (conversion or listed-building restoration only)
The property must be a single unit, with a minimum of 120 m² of main living area
Short-term letting of the Golden Visa property is PROHIBITED — the penalty is revocation and a €50,000 fine
There is NO minimum stay requirement
It covers the whole family (spouse, children under 21, dependent parents of both spouses)
Valid for 5 years, renewable for as long as the investment is maintained
Property transfer tax of 3% (plus a small municipal surcharge)
The VAT suspension on new-build has been extended to 31 December 2026 — the 3% transfer tax applies in such cases too

faq.q: How does the Greek Golden Visa work?
faq.a: By buying Greek real estate you can apply for a five-year, renewable residence permit that extends to the spouse, children under 21 and the dependent parents of both parties. The permit grants Schengen free movement and does not require minimum residence — it is enough to maintain the investment.

faq.q: What is the threshold of the Greek Golden Visa?
faq.a: There are three bands under Law 5100/2024. 800 000 € in the entire region of Attica, in the regional unit of Thessaloniki, on Mykonos, Santorini and every island with a population above 3 100. 400 000 € across all other parts of the country. 250 000 € only in two cases: if a non-residential building is converted into a home (the works must be completed before the application), or a listed heritage building is renovated. The 2024 transitional deadlines have all expired, so in 2026 the full 400/800 thousand threshold applies. Before buying, always check the specific zoning classification of the given property.

faq.q: Can I let my Greek Golden Visa property short-term?
faq.a: No. The 2024 reform expressly prohibits the short-term (Airbnb-style) letting of the property underlying the Golden Visa. Breaching the ban leads to withdrawal of the residence permit and a 50 000 euro fine. Long-term letting is permitted. This condition radically changes the yield calculation of the investment, yet it is missing from most offers.

faq.q: Does the Greek Golden Visa grant citizenship?
faq.a: Not directly. The Golden Visa is a residence permit; Greek citizenship can be applied for after seven years of actual residence in Greece, subject to language and integration conditions. If the goal is the passport itself, through real estate investment it is Turkey that grants citizenship directly.

ES.intro: Spain is Europe's highest-turnover holiday property market: the stock on the Costa del Sol (Marbella, Málaga), the Costa Blanca (Alicante), the Balearics and Barcelona is the deepest and most easily resold on the continent. Because of the international buyer base, the exit (sale) is substantially quicker than in smaller markets. One important legislative change: the property-based route into the Spanish Golden Visa was abolished in April 2025, so buying Spanish property today does NOT confer an investor residence permit — the purchase is purely an investment and lifestyle decision. Transfer tax ranges from 6% to 10% depending on the region.
highlights: Europe's largest and most liquid holiday property market — quick resale
Property transfer tax (ITP) of roughly 6–11% depending on the region: Madrid 6%, Andalusia 7%, Catalonia 10–11%
ATTENTION: the property-based Golden Visa was abolished in April 2025
An extensive Hungarian and international community along the southern coast

faq.q: Does the Spanish Golden Visa through property purchase still exist?
faq.a: No. Spain abolished the real-estate-investment route of the Golden Visa in April 2025, so a Spanish investor residence permit can no longer be obtained by buying property worth 500 000 euros. Within the EU, Greece and Hungary offer a property-based settlement programme; citizenship through real estate is granted primarily by Turkey.

faq.q: How much tax does the buyer pay in Spain?
faq.a: On a resale property the buyer is charged roughly 6–11% transfer tax (ITP) depending on the region: 6% in Madrid, 7% flat in Andalusia, 10% in Catalonia up to one million euros and 11% above, 10% in Valencia (9% from 1 June 2026, 11% above one million euros). The tax base is the higher of the purchase price and the official cadastral reference value — this is important because even with a low contract price the tax is levied on the reference value. On a newly built property bought from a developer, 10% VAT plus a region-dependent 0.5–1.5% stamp duty (AJD) is payable.

faq.q: What do you need as a Hungarian buyer to purchase Spanish real estate?
faq.a: A Spanish tax identification number (NIE), a Spanish bank account and typically an independent lawyer. As an EU citizen no purchase restriction applies to you, and the process concludes before a notary (escritura pública).

AE.intro: Dubai is the highest-turnover, entirely tax-free property market on the Persian Gulf: in the designated freehold zones (Dubai Marina, Downtown, Palm Jumeirah, Business Bay) a foreign buyer of any nationality can acquire full ownership in their own name, without a residence permit. There is no annual property tax, no personal income tax, no capital gains tax, and rental income earned by a private individual is not taxed either. Gross rental yields average around 7% across the city — outstanding by international standards — and a property worth 2,000,000 AED (~505,000 €) qualifies the owner for a renewable 10-year Golden Visa. Two things matter: outside the designated zones a foreigner cannot buy, so before purchase the classification of the specific property must be checked with the Dubai Land Department; and the UAE does NOT grant citizenship for property — only residence.
highlights: A foreigner of any nationality can buy FREEHOLD property in their own name in the designated zones, without a residence permit
No annual property tax, income tax, capital gains tax or rental tax for the private individual owner
Property transfer fee of 4% (officially 2% buyer + 2% seller; in practice the buyer pays the full amount)
From 2,000,000 AED (~505,000 €) a renewable 10-year Golden Visa — extends to family members too
The Golden Visa is RESIDENCE, not citizenship — the UAE does not grant a passport for property
Gross rental yield of ~7% on the city average; the dirham is pegged to the US dollar
Outside the designated zones a foreigner cannot buy — the zone must be verified with the DLD

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy property in Dubai?
faq.a: Yes. A foreign private individual of any nationality can buy full-ownership (freehold) property in their own name, without a residence permit, in Dubai's designated zones — such as Dubai Marina, Downtown, Palm Jumeirah and Business Bay. Outside these zones, however, ownership is reserved for UAE and GCC nationals, so before purchase it is advisable to check the classification of the specific property with the Dubai Land Department (DLD).

faq.q: How much tax is payable on a Dubai property?
faq.a: On purchase there is a 4% property transfer fee (DLD transfer fee) — officially 2% for the buyer and 2% for the seller, but in practice the buyer pays the full 4%. On top of this there is an approximately 4,200 AED trustee fee and a 250 AED title deed fee. Ownership, however, is effectively tax-free: there is no annual property tax, no personal income tax, no capital gains tax, and residential rental income earned by a private individual is not taxed either.

faq.q: Does Dubai grant a residence permit for buying property?
faq.a: Yes. A property worth at least 2,000,000 AED (~505,000 €) qualifies the owner for a renewable 10-year Golden Visa, which extends to the spouse, children (regardless of age) and parents, and carries no minimum stay requirement. Mortgaged property and, under certain conditions, off-plan property are also accepted. Important: this is a residence permit, NOT citizenship — the UAE does not grant a passport for property investment.

faq.q: Can you obtain citizenship in Dubai through property?
faq.a: No. The United Arab Emirates has no property-based citizenship programme. A passport can only be obtained through official nomination (for prominent investors, doctors, scientists and talents), which cannot be purchased and cannot be applied for. Through property investment in Dubai the 10-year Golden Visa (residence) is the most that is available; in our portfolio, citizenship through property is offered by Turkey.

faq.q: What rental yield can be achieved in Dubai?
faq.a: The gross rental yield on Dubai apartments averages around 7% across the city (according to independent data in mid-2026, roughly 6.9–7.2%), which is outstanding by international standards. In the more affordable, up-and-coming zones (for example Jumeirah Village Circle, Dubai Sports City) 7.5–8.5% is achievable, while in premium locations (Downtown, Palm Jumeirah) it is 5–6%. From the net yield the service charge must be deducted, which can be significant depending on the location.

allampolgarsag-ingatlanbefektetessel.title: Citizenship by property investment: where can you really earn a passport?
allampolgarsag-ingatlanbefektetessel.description: Which country grants citizenship directly for buying property? The thresholds, timelines and pitfalls of Turkey's citizenship-by-investment programme, and why no Golden Visa grants EU citizenship — a fresh 2026 overview.
allampolgarsag-ingatlanbefektetessel.answer: Citizenship by property investment — not merely residence — is available today with a large, liquid market primarily in Turkey: with property worth USD 400,000, extending to the whole family, typically within 3–6 months and with no residence requirement. A handful of Caribbean nations (Saint Kitts and Nevis, Dominica, Grenada, Antigua) also run property-based citizenship-by-investment programmes. Every EU route, by contrast — the Greek, the Hungarian or the former Spanish Golden Visa — grants only a residence permit, not citizenship and not a passport.

sections.h: The key distinction: citizenship or merely residence?
p: The market uses the term "Golden Visa" for two legally quite different things, and much of the confusion surrounding buying property abroad stems from exactly this. A residence programme (residence by investment) grants a settlement permit: you may live in the country, come and go, and in the case of an EU programme travel freely throughout the Schengen area — but your passport and your citizenship do not change. A citizenship programme (citizenship by investment, or CBI for short), on the other hand, grants genuine citizenship and a second passport, typically within a few months and without actually relocating.

This distinction is no hair-splitting: the two differ radically in price, timeline, tax consequences and the travel freedom they secure. Anyone who wants citizenship but buys a residence Golden Visa to get it can lose years and tens of thousands, because the two routes do not automatically lead into one another. So before committing to any programme, clarify one thing: is the goal a second passport, or merely the right to legal residence and free movement?

Direct citizenship through property investment, with a large, liquid market and a fast procedure, is offered today by Turkey — which is why this citizenship-by-investment route features in Proopify's portfolio. A few Caribbean nations (Saint Kitts and Nevis, Dominica, Grenada, Antigua and Barbuda) likewise run property-based citizenship programmes, but those are small, programme-driven markets with a slower and less certain exit. In the European Union there is NO such programme: Malta's citizenship programme had to be halted following the Court of Justice of the EU's 2025 ruling, while the Greek and Hungarian programmes are solely about residence permits.

img.alt: Citizenship by property investment: a modern Turkish apartment block that can reach the USD 400,000 citizenship-by-investment threshold

sections.h: Turkey: citizenship from property within 3–6 months
p: The world's highest-volume property-based citizenship programme belongs to Turkey. The condition: buying Turkish property worth at least USD 400,000, which must be held for three years — this is entered on the title deed (tapu) as a "satılamaz" (not for sale) annotation. The buyer then receives a short-term residence permit and subsequently citizenship, typically within 3–6 months. The programme extends to the spouse and children under 18; adult children and parents cannot be included in the same application.

The Turkish passport is strong in its own right: it allows visa-free or visa-on-arrival entry to roughly 110–120 countries and — unlike the Hungarian passport — creates eligibility to apply for the US E-2 investor visa. Citizenship remains even after the three-year holding period has elapsed and the property has been sold: Turkish citizenship is not revoked because of the sale. This is an important difference from residence programmes, where the right typically lapses if you withdraw the investment.

In practice the process looks like this: chosen property → SPK-licensed valuation → purchase price paid via bank transfer and documented → registration of title on the tapu with the not-for-sale clause → residence permit → citizenship application. Throughout, it is worth working with an independent lawyer who was not recommended by the developer.

head: Aspect
Turkey 🇹🇷
rows: Eligibility for the US E-2 investor visa

sections.h: The Caribbean alternative: faster, but a smaller and less liquid market
p: The Caribbean citizenship programmes (Saint Kitts and Nevis, Dominica, Grenada, Antigua and Barbuda) likewise accept investment into an approved property project — typically from USD 200,000–400,000 — in exchange for citizenship, often with a 4–8 month turnaround. These passports grant strong visa-free access, in many cases including the Schengen area and the United Kingdom (though visa-free status is a political decision and changes from time to time).

The Caribbean route's greatest weakness in an investor's eyes is the exit: these are small, programme-driven property markets where the approved projects can often be passed on only after several years of mandatory holding, on a limited secondary market, frequently at a loss. So anyone counting on recovering the investment is generally safer with Turkey's larger market of genuine demand — with a Caribbean programme it is better to regard the passport itself, and the discretion, as "the price".

sections.h: The pitfall the ads keep quiet about: the valuation
p: The most common mistake with the Turkish programme is that the buyer looks at the contract price. Yet that is not what matters — what matters is the official valuation by an SPK-licensed appraiser (approved by the capital markets authority) — and the USD 400,000 threshold must be met SIMULTANEOUSLY by the price paid and evidenced through a bank, by the valuation, and by the value stated on the title deed. A high contract price cannot "correct" a low valuation, and the threshold is measured in USD, so the exchange rate matters too: if the lira weakens between the sale and the valuation, the property's value measured in dollars can slip below the threshold.

The other common misunderstanding concerns the US E-2 visa. It is true that a Turkish citizen becomes eligible to apply for the E-2 investor visa. But the E-2 is not a settlement permit and not a green card; it is an investment-linked, renewable non-immigrant visa, and in practice naturalised citizens are expected to have held their citizenship for several years to use it — so it is not available the day after the Turkish passport is obtained.

Finally, the exit risk: at what REAL price can the property be passed on after the three-year holding period. Many new-build flats positioned specifically for the programme are sold above market price, with a "citizenship premium", and on the secondary market this premium disappears. This is the most frequently underestimated risk of CBI investments.

img.alt: Sea-facing Turkish property — for the citizenship-by-investment threshold it is the SPK valuation, not the contract price, that counts

sections.h: What to clarify before you decide
p: 1. Whether the valuation meets the threshold — in Turkey it is not the contract price but the official SPK valuation and the payment evidenced through a bank that count, measured in USD.
2. The tax consequences in your own jurisdiction: obtaining citizenship does not in itself terminate Hungarian tax residency, and does not exempt you from declaring your worldwide income at home.
3. The exit: at what real price, without a "citizenship premium", the property can be passed on to the secondary market after the three-year holding period.

4. Clarifying the goal: if the main aim is not a second passport but EU settlement and free movement, then you should be looking not for a citizenship-by-investment programme but for a Golden Visa — we cover this in a separate article.
This article is general information, not personalised legal or tax advice. Before a six-figure investment, always seek independent legal and tax expertise.

faq.q: In which country can you obtain citizenship by buying property?
faq.a: With a large, liquid property market and a fast procedure, primarily in Turkey: with property worth USD 400,000, extending to the whole family, typically within 3–6 months and with no residence requirement. A few Caribbean nations (Saint Kitts and Nevis, Dominica, Grenada, Antigua) also run property-based citizenship-by-investment programmes, but those are smaller, less liquid markets. In an EU member state you cannot obtain citizenship directly through property.

faq.q: Can you obtain EU citizenship through property investment?
faq.a: No. At present no EU member state allows you to obtain citizenship directly by buying property. The Greek and Hungarian programmes grant a residence permit, the Spanish property-based Golden Visa ended on 3 April 2025, and Malta's citizenship programme had to be halted following the Court of Justice of the EU's 2025 ruling.

faq.q: Do you have to keep the property after obtaining citizenship?
faq.a: Yes, for a time. In Turkey the property may not be sold for three years — this is entered on the title deed (tapu) as a "satılamaz" clause. Citizenship then remains even after the property is sold: Turkish citizenship is not revoked because of the sale.

faq.q: Will I lose my Hungarian citizenship if I become a Turkish citizen?
faq.a: No. Hungary permits dual citizenship, and Turkey does not require renouncing your previous citizenship either. The question of tax residency, however, is separate from this — it is worth clarifying with an expert versed in international tax matters.

faq.q: How much does Turkish citizenship through property cost in total?
faq.a: On top of the USD 400,000 property value, budget for the 4% tapu duty, the lawyer's and valuation fees, and the state processing fees per family member — this typically comes to a further 5–8% of the purchase price. The exact figure depends on the number of family members and the lawyer's fee schedule.

golden-visa-ingatlannal.title: Golden Visa through property investment in 2026: which country gives what?
golden-visa-ingatlannal.description: Greece, Hungary, Spain and the other Golden Visa programmes in 2026: thresholds, residence conditions, Greece's short-term letting ban, and which route actually leads to citizenship. A fresh, comparative overview.
golden-visa-ingatlannal.answer: A Golden Visa through property investment, by DIRECT property purchase, can today be obtained in the EU in practice only in Greece: from €400,000 across most of the country, from €800,000 in Attica, Thessaloniki and the popular islands. In the Hungarian guest-investor programme a direct home purchase does NOT qualify — there only an investment-fund unit (€250,000) or a higher-education donation (€1,000,000) counts. The Spanish property-based Golden Visa ended on 3 April 2025, and in Italy property is expressly excluded from the investor visa. If the goal is the passport itself, you need not a residence but a citizenship programme — through property this is offered primarily by Turkey.

sections.h: What does a Golden Visa actually give you?
p: A Golden Visa is a residence permit granted by the state in exchange for an investment — typically buying property. With an EU programme it also means Schengen free movement, so in most cases visa-free travel throughout the area, plus the inclusion of family members. What it does NOT give: a passport and citizenship. In most countries citizenship can be applied for only after several years of actual residence, a language exam and a naturalisation process — and the years spent on a Golden Visa typically do not even count towards this time if you do not actually live in the country.

As a Hungarian citizen you need no Golden Visa for free movement within the EU — which is why for domestic buyers these programmes are of interest mainly for tax-planning, business or "plan B" reasons, or where a third-country (non-EU) family member or business partner needs to be secured a residence right. Investment residence is thus rarely an end in itself for us; it is far more often one element of a broader wealth-planning or mobility strategy.

The link between the Golden Visa and property investment matters, too, because most programmes tie renewal of the permit to MAINTAINING the investment: if you sell the property, you typically lose the residence right as well. This is fundamentally different from citizenship programmes, where the citizenship acquired remains even after the property is sold.

img.alt: Golden Visa through property investment: a Greek seafront apartment block that can reach the €400,000 residence threshold

sections.h: The Golden Visa programmes side by side in 2026
p: The thresholds below show each country's official, property-based entry level in 2026. Important: the threshold alone says little — the short-term letting ban, the minimum floor area or the residence condition often affect the return on investment more than the entry sum itself.

head: Country
Threshold
What it gives
Residence
rows: 800 000 € (Attica, Thessaloniki, large islands) / 400 000 € (elsewhere) / 250 000 € (conversion only, listed building)
5-year, renewable residence permit, for the whole family
10-year permit — DIRECT home purchase does NOT qualify
ENDED: the property-based route closed on 3 April 2025
NONE: property is excluded from the investor visa

sections.h: Greece: the best-known EU Golden Visa programme
p: The Greek Golden Visa runs for five years, renewable as long as the investment remains, and extends to the spouse, children under 21 and the dependent parents of both partners. Its strongest feature is that there is NO minimum residence requirement: it is enough to keep the investment and renew the permit. This makes it the most popular EU investment-residence route, especially among Middle Eastern, Chinese and British buyers.

Since Law 5100/2024 the threshold has three bands. €800,000 across the whole of Attica, the regional unit of Thessaloniki, Mykonos, Santorini and every island with a population above 3,100. €400,000 in all other parts of the country. €250,000 in only two cases: converting a non-residential building into housing (the works must be completed BEFORE submitting the application), and renovating a listed building. The 2024 transitional deadlines have all lapsed, so in 2026 the full, raised threshold applies.

Two conditions that are missing from most offers, yet can decide the investment: in the €400,000 and €800,000 bands the property must be a SINGLE unit with at least 120 m² of main living space (the investment cannot be split across several small flats), and the property underlying the Golden Visa MUST NOT be let short-term, Airbnb-style. Breaching the ban leads to revocation of the permit and a €50,000 fine. For anyone who planned the Greek programme around tourist-letting yield, this fundamentally rewrites the calculation — in 2026 the Greek Golden Visa is more for long-term letting or personal use than for holiday letting.

sections.h: Hungary: the guest-investor programme relaunched in 2024
p: Here the most important thing is to clear up a widespread misunderstanding: in the Hungarian Guest Investor Programme a DIRECT HOME PURCHASE does NOT QUALIFY for a residence permit. The €500,000 direct-property option originally planned in the legislation was scrapped in January 2025, before it ever came into force. So anyone who simply buys a Budapest flat does not get a guest-investor permit — this is the most common myth about the Hungarian Golden Visa.

Two routes remain. One is buying a €250,000 investment-fund unit in a property fund registered with the Hungarian National Bank (MNB), whose net asset value is at least 40% in Hungarian residential property, with a five-year holding obligation. The other is a €1,000,000 non-refundable donation to a Hungarian higher-education institution. The permit runs for ten years and can be extended once by a further ten years — in terms of duration this is the longest in the EU — and the investment must be completed within 93 days of first entry on the guest-investor visa.

For a Hungarian and EU citizen this programme is naturally not relevant, since they enjoy free movement; its target audience is the third-country investor seeking a long, predictable EU residence right with a low residence burden.

sections.h: Spain and Italy: what many still get wrong
p: The property-based route of the Spanish Golden Visa (the classic €500,000 property purchase) was abolished on 3 April 2025. Anyone who obtained a Spanish Golden Visa earlier may keep their rights under transitional rules, but a NEW application via property can no longer be submitted. This makes many outdated offers still in circulation misleading — in 2026 investment residence through property cannot be obtained in Spain.

Italy's investor visa exists, but property is expressly EXCLUDED from it: eligibility is founded on government bonds (€2,000,000), capital invested in an Italian company (€500,000, or €250,000 for a start-up) or a philanthropic donation (€1,000,000) — buying a home does not count. Anyone who buys an Italian holiday home gets a fine property, but no residence right with it.

The lesson: the Golden Visa market changes fast, and old articles and ads regularly cite outdated thresholds and defunct programmes. Before investing, always confirm the conditions from the most up-to-date, official source (or from a local lawyer).

img.alt: Golden Visa programme 2026: the Spanish property-based route has ended and property is excluded from the Italian investor visa

sections.h: If the goal is the passport, don't look for a Golden Visa
p: The most common and most costly mistake is that someone wants citizenship and buys a Golden Visa to get it. The two are not the same and do not automatically lead into one another: Greek naturalisation, for example, requires seven years of ACTUAL residence in Greece, a language exam and an integration test — and the Golden Visa years, if you do not live there, do not count towards this.

If the real goal is a second passport, then you should choose not a residence but a citizenship (citizenship by investment) programme — through property this is offered primarily by Turkey, from USD 400,000, within 3–6 months. We covered this in detail in our article "Citizenship by property investment". So the correct order is always this: first the goal (passport or residence?), and only then the programme.

faq.q: In which country can you obtain a Golden Visa by buying property today?
faq.a: By direct property purchase, in practice only in Greece: from €400,000 across most of the country, from €800,000 in Attica, Thessaloniki and the popular islands, and from €250,000 only for building conversions or listed-building renovations. In the Hungarian programme a direct home purchase does not count — there you need a property-fund unit (€250,000) or a higher-education donation (€1,000,000). The Spanish property-based Golden Visa ended on 3 April 2025, and in Italy property is excluded from the investor visa.

faq.q: Does a Golden Visa grant EU citizenship?
faq.a: No. A Golden Visa is a residence permit. Citizenship can only be obtained through the usual naturalisation process, which typically requires several years of actual residence, a language exam and an integration test — seven years in the Greek case, for example.

faq.q: Do you have to live in the country to keep a Golden Visa?
faq.a: In the Greek and Hungarian programmes there is no minimum residence requirement: it is enough to keep the investment and renew the permit. This also means, however, that these years do not count towards the residence period required for naturalisation — Greek naturalisation, for example, requires seven years of ACTUAL residence in Greece.

faq.q: Can I let my Greek Golden Visa property short-term?
faq.a: No. Since the 2024 rules, the property underlying a Greek Golden Visa may not be let short-term, Airbnb-style; breaching the ban can lead to revocation of the permit and a €50,000 fine. Long-term letting or personal use is permitted — which is why in 2026 the programme is not for tourist yield.

faq.q: Is it worth buying a Golden Visa as a Hungarian citizen?
faq.a: Not for free movement in itself, since as a Hungarian citizen you are already entitled to it within the EU. It may be justified if a third-country family member needs a residence right, if there is a tax-planning or business purpose, or if the property investment stands up on its own merits — in which case the residence right is more of a bonus than the main reason.

kulfoldi-ingatlan-mellekkoltsegek.title: What does it really cost? Ancillary costs of buying property abroad in 12 countries
kulfoldi-ingatlan-mellekkoltsegek.description: Transfer tax, notary, lawyer, agency commission and currency conversion — the ancillary costs of buying property abroad, broken down by country. Total cost above the purchase price typically runs between 3% and 14%; we show where it is cheapest and where the most expensive.
kulfoldi-ingatlan-mellekkoltsegek.answer: The ancillary cost of buying property abroad, over and above the purchase price, differs significantly from country to country: in Albania there is effectively no ad valorem transfer tax, in Montenegro 3–6% (banded), in Hungary and Turkey 4%, while in Italy it is 9% (second home) and in Spain 6–10%. Added to this everywhere is the notary fee, the lawyer's fee and typically 2–5% agency commission, plus, in non-eurozone countries, the currency-conversion margin. The total ancillary cost therefore realistically comes to 5–14% of the purchase price.

sections.h: Why this matters more than the purchase price
p: When buying property abroad, most unpleasant surprises lie not in the price but in the ancillary cost. On top of the purchase price, another 5–14% typically comes into play, depending on the country — on a €300,000 property that is a difference of between €15,000 and €42,000 purely depending on which country you buy in. Many buyers notice this item at the last moment, whereas the whole budget should be planned around this gross figure from the outset, not around the advertised price.

The ancillary cost, moreover, is not a single item but consists of several independent elements: transfer/acquisition tax or VAT, notary fee, lawyer's fee, agency commission, cadastral registration fee, translation and certification, and — in a non-eurozone country — the cost of currency conversion. The table below shows the most important items by country; the exact amount always depends on the specific transaction.

One important basic rule: transfer tax typically applies to SECOND-HAND property — for new-build, in many countries VAT takes its place, which can be higher. So it must always be clarified whether the chosen property comes from a developer (VAT) or a private individual (transfer tax).

img.alt: Ancillary costs of buying property abroad: on top of the purchase price, transfer tax, notary, lawyer and agency commission of 5–14%

sections.h: Buyer's ancillary costs compared across 12 markets
p: The table below shows the most important buyer's items by country. The percentages are indicative bands applied to the purchase price; the fixed fees (the notary's base fee) weigh proportionately more on a smaller property.

sections.h: The cheapest and the most expensive entry
p: The most favourable transaction cost is offered by Albania, where the buyer pays no ad valorem transfer tax at all, followed by Thailand (2%) and Serbia (2.5%). In Croatia the duty is a flat 3%. Montenegro has applied a BANDED system since 2024: 3% up to €150,000, 5% above that, 6% above €500,000 — so on a €600,000 property the tax is not €18,000 but close to €28,000. At the other end of the scale stands Italy at 9% for a second home, and Spain, where the regional ITP (transfer tax) ranges roughly between 6% and 11% by region.

A common misconception is that the agency commission is always paid by the seller. This varies by market: in the Balkan and Turkish markets, splitting the commission or having the buyer bear it is also customary, so this must be clarified in writing BEFORE paying the deposit. If the contract is silent on this, a 2–3% item you did not plan for can easily end up on the buyer.

It is also worth comparing the markets from a currency angle: in eurozone or euro-priced countries (Montenegro, Croatia, Greece, Italy, Spain, Albania) a Hungarian buyer only has EUR/HUF conversion, whereas in Turkey, Thailand and Bali an additional currency comes in, whose bank conversion margin alone can be 1–3%.

sections.h: What to allow for beyond the table
p: Translation and certification: in many countries contracts must be submitted with a certified translation, sometimes with an apostille — this is a few hundred euros, but a mandatory item.
Tax number and bank account: in Spain a NIE, in Italy a codice fiscale, in Greece an AFM, in Turkey a vergi numarası is required — without these you cannot validly contract.
Annual upkeep: property tax, condominium service charge, insurance, and for a holiday property the winterisation or the management fee. In Dubai and Thailand the service charge can be notably high.

Currency conversion: in non-eurozone countries (Turkey, Thailand, Indonesia, Hungary) the bank conversion margin alone can be 1–3% — on a larger sum it is worth using a dedicated foreign-exchange provider rather than a commercial bank.

VAT on new-build property: on a new flat bought from a developer there is no separate transfer tax, but VAT is in the price (10% in Spain, 10–22% in Italy), which can be higher than the second-hand property's duty.

img.alt: Annual running costs of a property abroad: property tax, service charge and insurance on top of the buyer's ancillary costs

sections.h: Buying with a loan? Allow for the foreign buyer's extra burdens
p: If you finance the property purchase abroad partly with a loan, the ancillary cost grows further. Banks typically grant the foreign (non-resident) buyer a lower LTV (loan-to-value ratio) — often 50–70% instead of the local 80–90% — so a larger deposit is needed. Add to this the loan assessment and disbursement fee, the mandatory valuation, in many countries the mortgage registration duty, and often a required property or loan-cover insurance.

A foreign-currency loan is a separate risk: if the currency of the property and the loan differs from the currency of your income, an exchange-rate movement can also push up the repayment. This is why for most foreign buyers a cash (or domestically financed) purchase is simpler and more predictable — a local loan is only worthwhile if the interest and the associated costs together still stay below the yield.

In summary: always plan the total entry cost with a gross mindset — purchase price + transfer tax/VAT + notary + lawyer + agent + currency conversion + (where a loan is involved) the financing fees. This number, not the advertised price, is the real starting point.

faq.q: What is the total ancillary cost of buying property abroad?
faq.a: Depending on the country, typically 5–14% of the purchase price. It is lowest in Albania and Thailand (roughly 3–6%), moderate in Montenegro, Croatia, Hungary and Turkey (roughly 5–9%), and highest in Italy and Spain (roughly 10–14%). This includes the transfer tax, the notary, the lawyer and the agency commission.

faq.q: Who pays the agency commission when buying property abroad?
faq.a: It varies by market. In Western European markets it is typically the seller, but in the Balkan and Turkish markets splitting it or the buyer bearing it is also customary. Always clarify it in writing BEFORE paying the deposit, to avoid an unplanned 2–3% item.

faq.q: Do you have to pay transfer tax on new-build too?
faq.a: Usually not — in most countries the price of a new property bought from a developer includes VAT, and in that case there is no separate transfer tax. This is not necessarily cheaper, though: the Spanish 10% or the Italian 10–22% VAT can be higher than the transfer tax on a second-hand property.

faq.q: In which country is the buyer's ancillary cost lowest?
faq.a: In Albania, where the buyer pays no ad valorem transfer tax, so the total ancillary cost typically stays around 3–6%. This is followed by Thailand (2% transfer tax) and Serbia (2.5%). The most expensive are Italy (9% on a second home) and Spain (6–11% regional ITP).

faq.q: Does currency conversion count as an ancillary cost?
faq.a: Yes, in non-eurozone markets (Turkey, Thailand, Indonesia, Hungary) it is a meaningful item: the margin on a commercial bank conversion can be 1–3%, which on a €300,000 purchase is €3,000–9,000. With a dedicated international foreign-exchange provider this can be reduced to a fraction.

kulfoldi-ingatlanvasarlas-lepesei.title: Buying property abroad step by step: the full process in 2026
kulfoldi-ingatlanvasarlas-lepesei.description: From the search to the registration of title: how does buying property abroad work, step by step? Due diligence, deposit, preliminary contract, notary, tax number, power of attorney and the most common mistakes that cost money.
kulfoldi-ingatlanvasarlas-lepesei.answer: Buying property abroad consists of six steps: (1) the search and narrowing down by goal, (2) obtaining a local tax number and bank account, (3) legal due diligence on the title deed, carried out by an independent lawyer, (4) a preliminary contract and deposit (typically 10%, preferably into a lawyer's escrow), (5) the final sale contract before a notary and the documented payment of the purchase price, (6) registration of title in the cadastre. The whole process, for a cash purchase, typically takes 4–12 weeks.

sections.h: 1. The search and narrowing down by goal
p: The most common mistake in buying property abroad is that the buyer falls in love with the property, not the market. First you must decide the goal: rental yield, personal use, or a residence/citizenship programme. These three goals often justify a completely different country and a completely different property type, and if they become muddled, the property ends up serving none of them well.

For a yield goal, urban, year-round rental demand (Belgrade, Budapest, Bangkok) is more predictable than a seasonal coast. For personal use, flight accessibility and winter occupancy decide it. For a programme goal, the property's eligibility (whether it meets the Golden Visa or citizenship-by-investment threshold, whether it is a single unit, whether it is large enough) matters more than its location.

At this stage it is worth calculating a realistic total budget: purchase price + ancillary cost (5–14% depending on the country) + the first year's upkeep. If the budget only stretches to the advertised price, the transaction will stall at the ancillary cost.

img.alt: Buying property abroad step by step: in the search, first decide the goal — yield, personal use or Golden Visa

sections.h: 2. Local tax number and bank account
p: In almost every country a local tax number is needed for the contract: a NIE in Spain, a codice fiscale in Italy, an AFM in Greece, a vergi numarası in Turkey. Obtaining these typically takes 1–3 weeks, and in many cases can be arranged remotely by power of attorney — you do not necessarily have to travel out in person.

The local bank account is needed not only for the purchase price but also for later utilities, property tax and — with letting — receiving the income. Be prepared for anti-money-laundering (AML) checks: the source of the purchase price must be evidenced with documents (bank statement, a sale or employment certificate, an inheritance document). This is now standard at every serious bank, and its absence is the most common reason a transaction stalls.

sections.h: 3. Legal due diligence — the most expensive place to economise
p: Engage an independent lawyer who was NOT recommended by the seller or the agent. The minimum of legal due diligence: the title deed (who is the registered owner), charges and mortgages, the building and occupancy permit, the plot's zoning, service charge and tax arrears, and whether the flat's actual floor area matches the advertised one. This step decides whether you are buying a clean property or a legal risk.

For a still-under-construction (off-plan) property bought from a developer, a separate question is the bank guarantee or the escrow structure: what happens to the money paid in if the developer does not complete the construction. If there is no good answer to this, the deposit is at risk. In younger markets (Albania, for example) a property built without permits or with an unresolved title background is a real danger — here due diligence is not a formality but the most important step of the purchase.

sections.h: 4. Preliminary contract and deposit
p: The customary practice: alongside the preliminary contract a 10% deposit, which if the seller breaches the contract is typically returned double, but if the buyer withdraws without cause is forfeited. Pay the deposit preferably into a lawyer's or notary's escrow, not directly to the seller — this is the most important protection against fraud.

The preliminary contract must set out the deadline for the final contract, the exact purchase price and currency, the property's condition and fixtures (whether it is furnished, what stays in it), and who bears each ancillary cost. Every verbal promise (parking space, storeroom, refurbishment) must be put in writing — what is not in the contract does not, in practice, exist.

sections.h: 5. Final contract and payment
p: In most European markets the final sale takes place before a notary (the Spanish escritura, the Italian atto notarile, the Croatian/Montenegrin ugovor), who verifies the parties' identity and the title, but does NOT represent your interests — the substantive checking is the lawyer's job. In Turkey the transfer of ownership happens at the land registry, by re-registering the tapu.

Pay the purchase price preferably by bank transfer, in a documented way — cash payment is restricted or prohibited in several countries, and it makes evidencing the acquisition value harder at a later sale, which matters for capital-gains tax. On a foreign-currency purchase the timing of the exchange rate is money too: on a larger sum a dedicated foreign-exchange provider can save a lot on the bank margin.

sections.h: 6. Registration and follow-up tasks
p: Title becomes complete when the cadastre (property register) registers it. This takes anywhere from a few days to a few months, depending on the country. Then come re-registering the utilities, declaring the local property tax, and — if you let it — arranging the letting licence or the classification into a tourism category (in several countries, such as Greece and Croatia, this is a separate procedure).

Finally: settle your Hungarian taxation too. Rental income from a property abroad and the profit on a later sale are taxed under the double-taxation treaties — this differs by country, and it is not always the foreign tax that closes the question. We covered this in detail in our article "Taxation of property abroad as a Hungarian owner".

img.alt: Title becomes complete with cadastral registration — the last step of buying property abroad is arranging utilities and taxes

sections.h: The 6 most common mistakes that cost money
p: 1. Hiring the agent's lawyer — the most expensive economy. A lawyer recommended by the seller or the agent has a conflict of interest; you always need independent legal representation.
2. Deposit directly to the seller. Without escrow (a lawyer's/notary's account) the deposit is not protected — this is the most common point of fraud.
3. Planning only around the advertised price. The ancillary cost (5–14%) and the first year's upkeep are left out of the budget, and the transaction stalls in the end.
4. Not checking the floor area and the permits. The advertised and the actual square metres can differ; a part built without a permit may be impossible to legalise afterwards.
5. An off-plan purchase without a guarantee. If the developer's payment security or escrow is missing, the money paid in carries the developer's risk.
6. Cash payment without documentation. At a later sale, without evidence of the acquisition value the whole price may be treated as profit, which means higher tax.

faq.q: How long does buying property abroad take?
faq.a: Typically 4–12 weeks from the accepted offer to the registration of title, if it is a cash purchase. Obtaining the local tax number takes 1–3 weeks, the legal due diligence 1–2 weeks, and the notary closing depends on the parties' timing. With a loan, the process can be several weeks longer.

faq.q: Do you need to hire a lawyer when buying property abroad?
faq.a: Yes, and preferably one who was not recommended by the seller or the agent. In most countries the notary checks the legality of the contract, but does not represent your interests — checking charges, permits and floor area (due diligence) is the lawyer's job. This is the worst place to economise.

faq.q: Can you buy remotely, without appearing in person?
faq.a: In most countries yes, with a certified power of attorney bearing an apostille granted to a lawyer. In practice it is still worth viewing the property in person at least once — photographs are systematically prettier than reality, and the neighbourhood, the noise or the view can only be assessed on site.

faq.q: What is the most important thing to check in legal due diligence?
faq.a: The identity of the registered owner, the charges and mortgages, a valid building and occupancy permit, the plot's zoning, and whether the actual floor area matches the advertised one. For an off-plan (still-under-construction) property, additionally the developer's payment guarantee or escrow structure.

faq.q: Why should I not pay the seller directly?
faq.a: Because the deposit is then not protected. The correct solution is a lawyer's or notary's escrow: the money only reaches the seller once the contractual conditions are met. This protects against fraud and against losing the deposit in a dispute.

berleti-hozam-rangsor.title: Rental yield ranking 2026: where is it highest across 12 countries?
berleti-hozam-rangsor.description: In which country is it most worthwhile to buy property for letting? The gross rental yield of 12 markets in one table, with the difference between gross and net yield, seasonality, management cost and currency risk all flagged.
berleti-hozam-rangsor.answer: The highest gross rental yield is offered by Bali (6–9%), Dubai (~7%) and the emerging Balkan-Turkish urban markets (5–8%). The mature Western European markets (Italy, Spain) yield lower, 3–5%, but in exchange are more stable and liquid. The gross figure is misleading, though: the real NET rental yield, reduced by management and tax costs, is typically 2–4 percentage points lower, and with tourist letting the difference is greater still.

sections.h: Gross rental yield by country
p: Gross rental yield is the annual rental income divided by the purchase price (excluding ancillary costs). This is the most commonly advertised figure, because it looks best — but the real return is shown by the net yield, after deducting management, maintenance, tax and vacancy. The table shows the urban average, indicative gross yields; for a specific property it must always be recalculated, because location, floor and condition can move it by 2–3 percentage points.

The general rule: a higher gross yield comes with higher risk and stronger seasonality. The emerging and tourist markets (Bali, Albania, Dubai) show an attractive gross figure, but the income is uneven and management is expensive; the mature markets (Italy, Spain) give a lower but more predictable and more liquid yield.

head: Country
Gross yield
Character
rows: Urban, tax-free rental income; large supply

sections.h: Why net yield matters more than gross
p: With tourist (short-term) letting, the difference between gross and net rental yield is enormous. Professional villa or apartment management takes 15–20% of the income, on top of which come maintenance, cleaning, consumables, the platform commission (Airbnb/Booking 3–15%), insurance and — the least often planned item — off-season vacancy. In Bali, 6–9% gross realistically drops to 3–5% net by the time every item has come off it.

Long-term, urban letting (Belgrade, Budapest) is more predictable: the gross is lower, but the attrition is smaller, there is no seasonality risk, and the management cost is lower too. For a retirement or passive-income goal this is often the better choice, even if the advertised gross figure is less eye-catching.

The ancillary cost must be brought into the calculation too: the yield should really be applied to the purchase price + ancillary cost (5–14%), not just the purchase price. An 8% "gross" yield, applied to the total sum invested, is already closer to 7%, and net closer to 4–5%.

img.alt: Rental yield: with tourist letting there is a 2–4 percentage-point gap between gross and net yield because of management and vacancy

sections.h: Currency risk and liquidity: the two hidden factors
p: Currency risk matters: a yield outside the eurozone (Turkish lira, Thai baht, Indonesian rupiah) can be high in the local currency, but on conversion to the forint or the euro a weakening exchange rate can take away the profit. In Dubai the dirham is pegged to the dollar, which is more stable, but means USD exposure. In the eurozone markets (Montenegro, Croatia, Greece) a Hungarian buyer only has a EUR/HUF layer.

The second hidden factor is liquidity — that is, how quickly and at what price you can SELL the property. In a high-yield but small, emerging market (Albania, Bali) resale can drag on for months and the price is negotiable; in a mature market (Spain, Croatia) the exit is faster and more predictable. The total return is the combination of the rental yield AND the sale price — the two should never be viewed separately.
