sections.h: Short-term or long-term letting? Regulation decides too
p: Beyond the yield, the LEGAL framework of letting is also decisive, and it changes fast from market to market. In several popular target countries, short-term tourist letting is tied to a licence, registration or classification: in Greece and Croatia a separate tourism register and tax number are needed, and in some city centres a new Airbnb licence is temporarily not even issued. A Greek Golden Visa property, moreover, may not be let short-term at all.

This regulatory risk can matter even more than the gross yield: a market may show a 7–8% tourist yield, but if letting is tied to a licence or is being restricted, the plan will not materialise. So before calculating the yield, always clarify: in the given city and property, what letting is LEGALLY possible, with what licence and what tax.

The practical decision usually looks like this: tourist letting for a higher gross yield, but with strong seasonality, expensive management and regulatory risk; or long-term, year-round letting for a lower but stable and predictable yield. The two must be modelled for the specific property and city, not on a national average.

img.alt: Rental yield and regulation: short-term tourist letting is tied to a licence and registration in several countries

faq.q: In which country is the rental yield highest?
faq.a: On a gross basis Bali (6–9%), Dubai (~7%) and the Albanian coast (6–8%) lead. On a net basis, however, the urban, year-round markets (Belgrade, Budapest, 4–6% gross) often perform similarly well, because they have no seasonality risk and lower management costs.

faq.q: What is the difference between gross and net rental yield?
faq.a: With tourist letting, typically 2–4 percentage points. A Bali villa with an 8% gross yield is often 4–5% net. The difference comes from villa management (15–20%), platform fees (Airbnb/Booking), maintenance, cleaning, tax and vacancy.

faq.q: Does currency risk matter in letting?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. In non-eurozone countries (Turkey, Thailand, Indonesia, Hungary) the yield arises in the local currency, and on conversion to the forint or the euro the exchange-rate movement can take away or add several percent a year. In Dubai the dirham is pegged to the dollar, which is more stable, but means USD exposure.

faq.q: Is urban long-term or coastal tourist letting more worthwhile?
faq.a: The gross yield of tourist letting is higher, but the net falls a lot because of management, seasonality and vacancy, and the income is uneven. Urban long-term letting gives a lower gross, but is more stable, more predictable and cheaper to manage. For a passive-income goal, urban is often the more sensible.

faq.q: Should I calculate the yield on the purchase price or the total investment?
faq.a: On the total investment: purchase price + ancillary cost (5–14%) + any refurbishment. The advertised gross yield applies only to the purchase price, so it is more optimistic than reality. For a realistic return, the net rental income must be divided by the total sum invested.

faq.q: How do I calculate a realistic net rental yield before buying?
faq.a: Start from the realistic annual income (with tourist letting, reduced by off-season vacancy), deduct management (15–20%), the platform fee (Airbnb/Booking 3–15%), maintenance, cleaning, insurance and local tax, then apply the result not just to the purchase price but to the purchase price + ancillary cost (5–14%). The figure obtained this way will be well below the advertised gross yield, but this is the real return.

faq.q: Is tax-free rental income in Dubai really clear profit?
faq.a: Not necessarily. In Dubai there is indeed no personal income tax on rental income, but there is a high service charge, a management fee and vacancy, and on top of that, as a Hungarian tax resident the Hungarian side can count in or tax the income under the worldwide-income principle. Because of the dirham's USD peg, currency risk also remains.

legolcsobb-tengerparti-ingatlan-europa.title: The cheapest coastal property in Europe in 2026
legolcsobb-tengerparti-ingatlan-europa.description: Where can you buy a sea-view flat most cheaply in Europe in 2026? The per-square-metre prices of the Albanian Riviera, Montenegro's southern coast and the Balkans compared with the Croatian and Greek markets — entry prices, risks and legal pitfalls.
legolcsobb-tengerparti-ingatlan-europa.answer: The cheapest coastal entry in Europe is offered by Albania: on the Albanian Riviera (Ksamil, Sarandë) a new-build, sea-view flat is €1,200–2,200/m². This is followed by Montenegro's southern coast (Bar, Ulcinj) at around €1,500–2,500/m². Croatia and Greece are substantially more expensive (typically €2,500–6,000/m² in the good coastal locations), but in exchange come EU membership, Schengen free movement and a more liquid, more predictable market.

sections.h: Coastal entry prices compared
p: The per-square-metre price of coastal property varies strongly by distance from the shore, the view and the year of construction — in the same town a first-line, sea-view flat can be as much as double one a few streets back. The bands below apply to new-build or good-condition flats close to the sea (not necessarily first-line), for guidance.

The picture is clear: the cheapest coastal entry is in the southern, not-yet-EU part of the Balkans (Albania, Montenegro), and the most expensive in the mature, EU-member markets (Croatia, Greece). Between the two there is a difference not only in price but in legal predictability and liquidity — this must be weighed together when assessing the price advantage.

sections.h: Albania: the cheapest entry and its price
p: The Albanian Riviera (Ksamil, Sarandë, Vlorë) offers Europe's cheapest new-build sea-view flats, often in the €1,200–2,200/m² band — that is a half to a third of comparable Croatian or Greek stock. The buyer's ancillary cost is also the lowest, because in Albania there is no ad valorem transfer tax on the buyer's side, and the rental yield is high thanks to the emerging tourism (6–8% gross).

The low price has a price of its own, however: the market is the youngest, and properties with an unresolved title background or built without permits do occur. Without due diligence carried out by an independent lawyer — the title deed in the ASHK cadastre, the valid building permit, the plot's zoning — never pay a deposit here. Anyone who skips this step can turn the cheapest entry into the most expensive mistake.

img.alt: The cheapest coastal property in Europe: on the Albanian Riviera a new-build, sea-view flat is €1,200–2,200/m²

sections.h: Montenegro: the good middle ground
p: Montenegro is a good compromise between price and security: you pay in euros (though it is not an EU member), the transfer duty is among the lowest (3% up to €150,000), and the foreign buyer purchases a flat on effectively the same rights as locals. The southern coast (Bar, Ulcinj) is still affordable (€1,500–2,500/m²), the premium Bay of Kotor (Budva, Kotor) is more expensive, but has strong, year-round demand.

In exchange, Montenegro is not an EU member (though an active candidate, with a target date around 2028), which matters for Schengen free movement and legal harmonisation. Anyone building on EU membership and the predictability that comes with it should weigh this; anyone for whom euro settlement, the low entry and the high coastal demand matter will often find Montenegro the best value-for-money choice in the region.

sections.h: More expensively in an EU member state: when is it worth it?
p: Croatia and Greece are substantially more expensive (€2,500–6,000/m² in the good coastal locations), but come with EU membership, Schengen free movement and substantially more liquid resale. Greece, moreover, also offers a Golden Visa option (from €400,000), and Croatia has been a eurozone and Schengen member since 2023, which eliminates both currency risk and border controls.

The decision ultimately hinges on priorities. If the goal is maximum yield or the lowest entry, Albania and Montenegro are better. If Schengen free movement, legal predictability and a fast, predictable exit matter more, then the Croatian or Greek premium price is worth it — because a cheap property that is hard to sell easily eats up the price advantage shown on paper.

sections.h: What is the real entry for a 60 m² near-sea flat?
p: Let us translate the per-square-metre price into a concrete entry. A 60 m² new flat close to the sea on the Albanian Riviera, in the €1,200–2,200/m² band, is roughly €72,000–132,000; on Montenegro's southern coast (€1,500–2,500/m²), €90,000–150,000; in Croatian Dalmatia (€2,500–5,000/m²), already €150,000–300,000; on the Greek islands (€2,500–6,000/m²), €150,000–360,000. Added to this is the buyer's ancillary cost, which is lowest precisely in Albania.

The lesson is twofold. On the one hand, the price advantage is real and large: for the same flat size, in the southern part of the Balkans a half to a third of the Croatian-Greek price is enough. On the other hand, the cheapest entry is in the youngest, least liquid markets, where legal due diligence and an exit plan matter more than anywhere. The good decision is not the cheapest price but the best price-to-risk ratio — aligned with the given goal (yield, personal use, resaleability).

faq.q: Where is the cheapest coastal property in Europe?
faq.a: In Albania, on the Albanian Riviera (Ksamil, Sarandë): new-build, sea-view flats range in the €1,200–2,200/m² band, which is a half to a third of comparable Croatian or Greek stock. The buyer's ancillary cost is also the lowest, because in Albania there is no ad valorem transfer tax on the buyer's side.

faq.q: Is it safe to buy coastal property cheaply in Albania?
faq.a: Legally yes — a foreigner may buy a flat in their own name — but the main risk is the title background: properties built without permits or with an unresolved title do occur. Due diligence carried out by an independent lawyer (the title deed in the ASHK cadastre, the building permit) is mandatory before paying the deposit.

faq.q: Is it worth buying coastal property more expensively in an EU member state?
faq.a: If Schengen free movement, legal predictability and fast resale matter, then yes — Croatia and Greece are more liquid and more predictable. If the goal is maximum yield or the lowest entry, Albania and Montenegro give better value for money.

faq.q: Why is the Montenegrin coast cheaper than the Croatian?
faq.a: Primarily because Montenegro is not yet an EU member, its market is smaller and less liquid, and the southern coast is developing. In exchange, you pay in euros and it is among the lowest for duties (from 3%). The premium Bay of Kotor (Kotor, Budva) is already approaching Dalmatian levels on price.

faq.q: How much does a sea-view flat in Montenegro cost?
faq.a: On the southern coast (Bar, Ulcinj) typically €1,500–2,500/m², in the premium Bay of Kotor (Budva, Kotor) €2,500–4,500/m². A sea-view, first-line flat is in the upper band or above it; a few streets back the price is noticeably lower.

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy coastal property in their own name in the Balkans?
faq.a: In most Balkan markets yes: in Montenegro and Albania a foreign private individual may buy a flat in their own name on effectively the same rights as locals. In Croatia an EU citizen buys freely; in some cases there may be a restriction on agricultural or special land. The rule must always be checked for the specific property type before the deposit.

faq.q: Is cheaper coastal property a worse investment?
faq.a: Not in itself — but it has a different risk profile. The cheap entry (Albania, the southern Montenegrin coast) can offer a higher yield and greater price-growth potential, in exchange for a younger, less liquid market and a greater due-diligence requirement. The more expensive EU market (Croatia, Greece) gives a lower yield but a more predictable exit. The good choice depends on the goal.

kulfoldi-ingatlan-adozasa-magyar-tulajdo.title: Taxation of property abroad as a Hungarian owner in 2026
kulfoldi-ingatlan-adozasa-magyar-tulajdo.description: How is the rental income and sale profit of a property abroad taxed for an owner with Hungarian tax residency? The logic of double-taxation treaties, the exemption and credit methods, and the foreign-currency tax base explained clearly.
kulfoldi-ingatlan-adozasa-magyar-tulajdo.answer: For a private individual with Hungarian tax residency, the income of a property abroad can arise twice over: in the country where the property is located AND in Hungary (because of the worldwide-income principle). Double taxation is resolved by the treaties Hungary has concluded — with property, almost always the country of LOCATION taxes primarily, and Hungary either exempts or credits the tax paid abroad. The specific rule differs by country, so before investing it is worth clarifying it with an international tax adviser.

sections.h: The key concept: tax residency
p: A Hungarian citizen typically remains a Hungarian tax resident even if they own property abroad — unless they actually relocate and move the centre of their vital interests to another country. A Hungarian-resident private individual is taxed in Hungary on their WORLDWIDE income, so the rental income and sale profit of a property abroad also fall within Hungarian taxation. Double tax is not eliminated by the fact that you have already paid abroad, but by the double-taxation treaty between the two countries.

An important and often misunderstood point: the fact that you obtain citizenship (citizenship by investment) or a Golden Visa abroad does NOT in itself terminate your Hungarian tax residency. Residency is tied to actual presence and the centre of interests, not to the passport. Even holding a Turkish passport or a Greek Golden Visa, you can remain a Hungarian resident, and your worldwide income is declarable at home too.

img.alt: Taxation of property abroad as a Hungarian owner: a Hungarian-resident individual is taxed on worldwide income, the treaty resolves double tax

sections.h: Rental income: exemption or credit
p: The rental income of a property abroad is, under almost every treaty, taxed primarily by the country where the property is located. Hungary can then proceed in one of two ways, depending on the treaty in question. Under the EXEMPTION method, the rental income taxed abroad is not taxed again at home (though it may be taken into account with progression retention when setting the tax rate on other income). Under the CREDIT method, Hungary also calculates the tax, but deducts from it what has already been paid abroad.

The two can give very different results, especially where the country of location taxes a private individual's rental income little or not at all. In the United Arab Emirates (Dubai), for example, there is no personal income tax — in which case it is especially important to check whether the Hungarian side exempts or credits, because under exemption the income effectively remains tax-free, whereas under credit the full Hungarian tax must be paid (since there is nothing to credit).

This is why one cannot say generally that "letting abroad is taxed like this" — you must always look at the SPECIFIC country's treaty. The same 6% gross yield can produce a different net under the treaties of two different countries.

sections.h: Sale (capital) gain and the foreign-currency tax base
p: The profit from selling the property is likewise, under the treaties, typically left to the country of location to tax. In Hungary, income from selling a domestic property can be tax-free from the fifth year after the year of acquisition, but for a property abroad you must always look at the specific treaty and the country-of-location rule TOGETHER — you cannot automatically rely on the Hungarian five-year rule.

In the case of a foreign-currency purchase and sale, the exchange rate is part of the picture too: the Hungarian tax base must be determined in forints, so the difference between the exchange rate at purchase and at sale in itself affects the calculated profit. It can happen that the property has barely appreciated in the given currency, but because of the forint's weakening a significant profit nonetheless arises in forints — or vice versa. The purchase price paid by documented bank transfer is crucial here, because without evidence of the acquisition value the whole sale price may be treated as profit.

sections.h: Practical steps and responsibility
p: In practice, the Hungarian-resident owner must: (1) clarify whether there is a treaty with the given country and which method (exemption/credit) it applies, (2) document the foreign rental income and the foreign tax paid, (3) include it in the Hungarian personal income tax return under the worldwide-income principle, (4) for foreign-currency items, convert to forints at the MNB exchange rate.

This article is general information, not personalised tax advice. International property taxation is complex, changes frequently, and on a six-figure investment a badly chosen structure can cost a lot of money — so before investing, always seek the opinion of an adviser versed in international tax matters.

sections.h: Inheritance, gifting and local wealth taxes
p: Beyond the rental income and the sale profit, a property abroad has two often-forgotten tax layers. One is the LOCAL annual holding tax: many countries have a property tax or wealth tax (the Spanish IBI, the Greek ENFIA, the Italian IMU on a second home) that is due for the mere fact of ownership, regardless of whether you let it. In the yield calculation this reduces the net result, and it is often left out of the planning.

The other layer is inheritance and gifting. Passing a property abroad down typically also falls under the country-of-location's inheritance/gift rules, and in some countries (Spain, for example, varying regionally) this can be a meaningful burden. The relationship between the Hungarian and the local rule, and any treaty, must be viewed together here too — a well-designed ownership structure (who owns it and in what proportion) can make a lot of difference.

For exactly this reason, the taxation of a property abroad is not enough to think through in the year of purchase: the annual taxes during holding, the rental income, the eventual sale and the inheritance together make up the full picture. This article is general information; for the specific structure you need an international tax adviser.

img.alt: Taxation of property abroad: the annual local wealth tax (Spanish IBI, Greek ENFIA, Italian IMU) and inheritance are also part of the total tax burden

faq.q: Do I have to pay tax in Hungary on my property abroad?
faq.a: As a Hungarian tax resident you are taxable at home on your worldwide income, but the double-taxation treaty prevents the same thing being taxed twice. With property, typically the country of location taxes primarily, and Hungary either exempts the income or credits the tax paid abroad. The specific method differs by treaty.

faq.q: Does citizenship or a Golden Visa terminate my Hungarian tax liability?
faq.a: No. Tax residency is tied to actual presence and the centre of interests (the centre of vital interests), not to the passport. If you do not relocate and do not move the centre of your life, you remain a Hungarian resident, and your worldwide income is taxable at home too.

faq.q: What is the difference between the exemption and the credit method?
faq.a: Under the exemption method, Hungary does not tax again the income taxed abroad (at most it takes it into account with progression retention). Under the credit method, Hungary also calculates the tax, but deducts from it what has been paid abroad. Where the country of location taxes little (e.g. Dubai), the two give very different end results.

faq.q: Will the sale of a property abroad be tax-free after 5 years?
faq.a: Not automatically. The Hungarian five-year exemption rule applies to domestic property; for one abroad you must always look at the specific treaty and the country-of-location rule together. The country of location can tax the sale profit under its own rule, regardless of the Hungarian five years.

faq.q: Does the exchange rate matter for the tax on a property abroad?
faq.a: Yes. The Hungarian tax base must be determined in forints, so the difference between the exchange rate at purchase and at sale can in itself cause a profit or a loss. It can happen that the property has barely appreciated in the currency, but because of the forint's weakening a taxable profit nonetheless arises in forints.

deviza-arfolyamkockazat-kulfoldi-ingatla.title: Currency and exchange-rate risk when buying property abroad
deviza-arfolyamkockazat-kulfoldi-ingatla.description: Euro, dollar, lira or dirham? Which currency the risk is priced in across the 12 markets, and how you can save on currency conversion when buying property abroad. The bank margin, the FX providers and the Turkish-lira pitfall.
deviza-arfolyamkockazat-kulfoldi-ingatla.answer: Half of the 12 markets are eurozone or euro-priced (Montenegro, Croatia, Greece, Italy, Spain, Albania), so a Hungarian buyer has only EUR/HUF exchange-rate risk. In the rest, an additional currency comes in: the Dubai dirham is pegged to the dollar (stable, but USD exposure), while the Turkish lira, the Thai baht and the Indonesian rupiah fluctuate. The margin on bank currency conversion can be 1–3% in itself — on this, a dedicated FX provider can save a lot on a larger sum.

sections.h: What is the currency exposure in each market?
p: Currency risk strikes at two points when buying property abroad: when paying the purchase price (a one-off, large conversion) and later when remitting the rental income home (a recurring conversion). In the eurozone markets a Hungarian buyer has only one currency layer (EUR/HUF), in the non-eurozone ones two — between the local currency and the forint, often via an intermediate dollar.

The table below shows the currency and the actual exposure of a Hungarian/euro-based buyer by market. The logic is simple: the more conversion layers and the more volatile the local currency, the greater the hidden exchange-rate risk on the yield and the capital.

sections.h: How to reduce the conversion loss?
p: The commercial banks' currency-conversion margin (the difference between the buy and sell rate) is a serious sum on a larger property purchase: at €300,000 a 2% margin is €6,000, a 3% margin already €9,000. This is a pure loss for which you get nothing in return. With a dedicated FX provider or an international transfer platform this margin can be reduced to a fraction — on a larger sum this is one of the most easily saved items in the whole transaction.

Practical tips: request a rate from several providers for the same amount and time (the difference can be surprisingly large); watch out for HIDDEN fees, because "0% commission" is often hidden in a worse exchange rate; and on a large, time-able sum it is worth not doing the conversion in a single, rushed transaction. The goal is always to compare the effective exchange rate, not the advertised commission.

The Dubai dirham has been pegged to the dollar (1 USD = 3.6725 AED) since 1997, which is stable — but a Hungarian or euro-based buyer is actually taking on USD risk, because the EUR/USD movement acts on the return through the dirham. A "stable currency" therefore does not mean risk-free for a forint- or euro-based investor.

img.alt: Currency conversion when buying property abroad: the bank margin is 1–3%, which a dedicated FX provider can reduce to a fraction

sections.h: The Turkish-lira pitfall: when the exchange rate also affects eligibility
p: The Turkish citizenship-by-investment programme is a special case, because here the currency affects not only the cost but also ELIGIBILITY. The USD 400,000 citizenship threshold is measured in dollars, but the purchase price flows in lira. If the lira weakens between signing the contract and the SPK valuation, the property's value measured in dollars can fall and drop below the threshold — that is, because of an exchange-rate movement the investment can lose its eligibility for citizenship.

So with the Turkish programme the currency timing and fixing are critical: the purchase price and the valuation must be kept close in time, and it is worth leaving a buffer above the threshold so that an exchange-rate movement does not sink the eligibility. This kind of "double" currency risk (lira for the payment, dollar for the threshold) is one of the least communicated pitfalls of Turkish property-based citizenship.

sections.h: Remitted rental income: the recurring exchange-rate risk
p: With currency risk most people think only of the one-off, large purchase-price conversion, yet for landlords the RECURRING conversion matters at least as much. If the property is non-eurozone (Turkey, Thailand, Bali), the rental income arises in the local currency, and with every remittance home it must be converted anew to forints or euros. A persistently weakening local currency (like the Turkish lira historically) can melt away, year after year, a fine yield in the local currency once expressed in forints.

There is partial protection against this: a rental contract priced in euros or dollars where this is customary (in many emerging markets premium tenants accept it), aligning the currency of the income with the currency of the costs, and not carrying out the remittance under duress, at a bad rate. The point is that currency risk must be built into the yield plan from the outset, not confronted afterwards.

In summary: the return on a property abroad is decided at three currency points — the conversion of the purchase price, the recurring conversion of the rental income and the final conversion of the sale price. In a eurozone market all three are simple (EUR/HUF); in a non-eurozone one all three are separate risks that must be managed consciously.

faq.q: Which currency is the safest for buying property abroad?
faq.a: For a Hungarian or euro-based buyer, the eurozone or euro-priced markets (Montenegro, Croatia, Greece, Italy, Spain, Albania) are the most predictable, because there is only one conversion layer (EUR/HUF). The Dubai dirham is stable (pegged to the dollar), but carries USD exposure; the Turkish lira, the Thai baht and the Indonesian rupiah are more volatile.

faq.q: How much can you save on currency conversion?
faq.a: The commercial bank conversion margin is 1–3%. On a €300,000 purchase that is €3,000–9,000. With a dedicated international FX provider the margin can be reduced to a fraction, so on a larger sum it is always worth comparing several offers based on the effective exchange rate, not the advertised commission.

faq.q: Why is the Turkish lira risky for the Golden Visa?
faq.a: The citizenship threshold (USD 400,000) is measured in dollars, but the purchase price flows in lira. If the lira weakens between the purchase and the valuation, the property's value measured in dollars can fall and drop below the threshold — which is why currency timing and a buffer above the threshold are critical here.

faq.q: Is the dollar-pegged dirham risk-free for a euro-based buyer?
faq.a: No. The dirham is pegged to the dollar (1 USD = 3.6725 AED), so it is stable against the dollar — but a euro- or forint-based investor is thereby actually taking on USD risk, because the EUR/USD (or USD/HUF) movement acts on the yield and the capital through the dirham.

faq.q: How do I choose an FX provider for a property purchase?
faq.a: Compare the EFFECTIVE exchange rate (not the advertised commission) across several providers for the same amount; watch out for hidden fees behind "0% commission"; and on a large, time-able sum do not do it in a single rushed conversion. The goal is to minimise the total conversion cost.

faq.q: Is it worth buying in a eurozone country because of currency risk?
faq.a: For a Hungarian or euro-based buyer, often yes: in the eurozone or euro-priced markets (Montenegro, Croatia, Greece, Italy, Spain, Albania) there is only a single conversion layer (EUR/HUF), so the yield and the capital are more predictable. A higher local-currency yield (lira, baht, rupiah) can be attractive, but exchange-rate weakening can take away the profit year after year.

faq.q: When should I convert a large sum for a property purchase?
faq.a: On a large, time-able sum do not convert in a single rushed transaction at the wrong moment; compare several dedicated FX providers based on the effective exchange rate, and where possible tie the conversion to the contract's payment deadline, so that you do not have to hold an unnecessary buffer in a volatile currency. The goal is to minimise the total conversion cost.

ingatlanszotar-fogalmak.title: Property glossary: 22 terms to know before buying abroad
ingatlanszotar-fogalmak.description: Freehold, Golden Visa, tapu, escrow, off-plan, NIE, due diligence — the most important terms of buying property abroad, explained briefly and clearly. Misunderstood terms cost money.
ingatlanszotar-fogalmak.answer: Buying property abroad is full of technical terms whose misunderstanding costs money. The most important: freehold is full (perpetual) ownership, leasehold is a long-term lease right; the Golden Visa is RESIDENCE for investment, while CBI (citizenship by investment) is CITIZENSHIP; escrow is the deposit account that protects the deposit; off-plan is a still-under-construction property; due diligence is legal vetting. This glossary explains 22 terms, in the areas of ownership, immigration, the purchase process and taxation.

sections.h: Ownership and law
p: The first linguistic obstacle in buying property abroad is understanding the forms of ownership — especially because in Asia a foreigner often CANNOT acquire full ownership, only a limited right. The terms below cover this layer.
Freehold — full, unlimited-in-time ownership (the equivalent of the Hungarian "tulajdon"); the property, and typically the land too, belong to the buyer.

Leasehold — a long-term lease/use right (e.g. 25–99 years); not ownership but a right for a fixed term. In Bali and Thailand foreigners typically acquire this, so here the renewal condition is the most important thing to check.
Usufruct (right of use and enjoyment) — the right to use the property and take its benefits, without transfer of ownership; in Thailand foreigners often make use of it.
Hak Pakai / Hak Sewa / Hak Guna Bangunan — the Indonesian rights of use, lease and building that a foreigner can acquire instead of freehold in Bali.
Cadastre / title deed — the official property register; ownership becomes complete when registered here. In Turkey this is the "tapu", in Albania the ASHK.
Tapu — the Turkish title deed (deed of ownership); the transfer of ownership becomes legally complete at the land registry by re-registering the tapu.

img.alt: Property glossary for buying abroad: freehold, leasehold, usufruct, tapu and cadastre — the forms of ownership are the most important terms

sections.h: Immigration and investment
p: Golden Visa — a RESIDENCE permit granted for investment (typically buying property); not citizenship and not a passport. Renewal of the permit is typically tied to maintaining the investment.

CBI (citizenship by investment) — CITIZENSHIP and a passport granted for investment; through property this is offered today primarily by Turkey (from USD 400,000). The citizenship acquired remains even after the property is sold.
Residence by investment — the official, broader name for the Golden Visa: a residence right acquired through investment, as opposed to citizenship by investment.
Due diligence — legal vetting: checking the title deed, charges, permits and floor area before the purchase, with an independent lawyer.
E-2 visa — a US investor (non-immigrant) visa, for which a Turkish citizen can become eligible; not a green card and not settlement.

sections.h: The purchase process
p: Escrow / deposit account — a neutral (lawyer's or bank) account that holds the deposit or the purchase price until the contractual conditions are met; protects against fraud, especially in an off-plan purchase.
Off-plan — a still-under-construction property bought on paper (from the plans); can be cheaper, but carries developer and completion risk — here the escrow and the bank guarantee are crucial.
Preliminary contract — the contract preparing the final sale, typically with a 10% deposit; sets out the deadline and conditions of the final contract.
Deposit — the sum securing the contract, which if the seller breaches the contract is typically returned double, but if the buyer withdraws without cause is forfeited.
Snagging — the defect list before handover (the schedule of shortcomings to be remedied by the developer on a new-build).
Service charge — the condominium common charge (significant in Dubai and Thailand, it can be a meaningful item on an annual basis).
Apostille — a form of international document certification; often needed for a foreign power of attorney and documents.

sections.h: Tax and finance
p: Transfer / acquisition tax — the tax payable on the transfer of ownership (e.g. the Turkish tapu duty, the Spanish ITP); typically applies to second-hand property.
ITP / AJD — the Spanish transfer tax (ITP) and the stamp duty (AJD); with regionally varying rates.
Prezzo-valore — the Italian mechanism whereby, between private individuals, the tax base is the (typically lower) cadastral value, not the actual purchase price.
NIE / codice fiscale / AFM — the Spanish, Italian and Greek foreign tax number, without which you cannot validly contract; obtaining it takes 1–3 weeks.
LTV (loan-to-value) — the ratio of the loan to the property value; banks typically grant a foreign buyer a lower LTV (a larger deposit).
Double-taxation treaty — the agreement between two countries that decides which country taxes, and how it can be avoided that the same thing is taxed twice.
Worldwide-income principle — a Hungarian-resident private individual is taxed at home on their entire (domestic and foreign) income; the income of a property abroad also belongs here.

sections.h: Letting, yield and exit
p: Gross rental yield — the annual rental income divided by the purchase price; the most commonly advertised metric, but one that ignores the costs.
Net rental yield — the real yield after deducting management, maintenance, tax and vacancy; with tourist letting typically 2–4 percentage points below the gross.
Liquidity — how quickly and at what price the property can be sold; lower in emerging markets, higher in mature ones.
Yield gap — the difference between the gross and the net yield; the greater the management and seasonal burden, the larger it is.
Property management — the professional handling of letting (bookings, cleaning, maintenance); with tourist letting it can take as much as 15–20% of the income.
Short-term letting (short-let) — tourist, daily/weekly letting (Airbnb, Booking); a higher yield, but seasonal and tied to a licence in several countries.
NAP consistency — the matching of name, address and phone number across the various registers; important for local online visibility and Google results.

faq.q: What is the difference between freehold and leasehold?
faq.a: Freehold is full, unlimited-in-time ownership. Leasehold is a fixed-term (typically 25–99-year) lease or use right, which at the end of the term expires or must be renewed. In Bali and Thailand foreigners can often acquire only leasehold — in which case the renewal condition is the most important thing to check.

faq.q: Is the Golden Visa the same as citizenship?
faq.a: No. The Golden Visa is a residence permit granted for investment — you may live and come and go in the given country, but your passport does not change. Citizenship (CBI, citizenship by investment) gives a genuine passport. The two are often confused; through property, citizenship is offered today primarily by Turkey.

faq.q: What is escrow, and why is it important?
faq.a: Escrow is a neutral deposit account (a lawyer's or bank's) that holds the deposit or the purchase price until the contractual conditions are met. This protects against fraud and against the developer's non-performance, which is why especially in an off-plan (still-under-construction) purchase it is crucial that the money goes into escrow, not directly to the seller.

faq.q: What is an off-plan purchase, and what is its risk?
faq.a: Off-plan is a still-under-construction property bought from the plans. It can be cheaper and can capture the appreciation during development, but it carries developer and completion risk: if the developer does not finish, the money can be put in danger. This is why the bank guarantee or the escrow structure is the most important thing to check here.

faq.q: Why do you need a NIE, codice fiscale or AFM?
faq.a: These are the foreign tax numbers (Spain: NIE, Italy: codice fiscale, Greece: AFM), without which you cannot validly acquire property, open a bank account or re-register utilities. Obtaining them typically takes 1–3 weeks, and in many cases can be arranged remotely by power of attorney.
